message.content:
You are confirmer #1, independent. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correct, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it; for a code change the named regression test must DISCRIMINATE — fail if the fix were reverted). Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix G-024 in src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py
What: corrected docstring: determinism freeze copies array bytes O(size), not zero-copy/O(1).
Test(s): (doc-accuracy)
Steps: read the actual changed file(s); read the test and judge whether it discriminates; check no regression. For a doc/refactor item, judge correctness/coverage rather than revert-discrimination. Verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested). Be skeptical and specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

100	        # finite inputs can still overflow when squared (||x||) on huge-magnitude vectors;
101	        # reject rather than silently produce inf/inf similarities (audit N1; restores parity
102	        # with the pure path, which raises on the same input).
103	        raise ValueError("vector norm overflow — component magnitudes are too large")
104	    zero = norms == 0.0
105	    safe = np.where(zero, 1.0, norms)
106	    xn = x / safe[:, None]
107	    m = xn @ xn.T
108	    np.clip(m, -1.0, 1.0, out=m)
109	    if zero.any():  # zero vectors have no direction → similarity 0 to everything
110	        m[zero, :] = 0.0
111	        m[:, zero] = 0.0
112	    np.fill_diagonal(m, np.where(zero, 0.0, 1.0))
113	    return m
114

116	def _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
117	    return [[float(v) for v in row] for row in _cosine_ndarray(rows)]
118	
119	
120	def cosine_similarity_matrix_array(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> Any:
121	    """The cosine matrix as a numpy ndarray — no nested-list boxing (#106).
122	
123	    For large corpora the nested-list form of :func:`cosine_similarity_matrix` multiplies peak
124	    memory several-fold; a builder operating at scale should keep the ndarray and store it in the
125	    baseline's ``contents``. The determinism freeze still copies the array's bytes once
126	    (``freeze`` -> ``tobytes()`` into a ``(kind, shape, bytes)`` descriptor, O(size)) — far cheaper
127	    than freezing a deep nested list element-by-element, but NOT zero-copy/O(1) (pass 4, #G-024).
128	    Requires numpy (the ``baseline`` extra).
129	    """
130	    try:
131	        import numpy as np
132	    except ImportError as exc:  # pragma: no cover - exercised only without the extra
133	        raise RuntimeError(
134	            "cosine_similarity_matrix_array requires numpy (the 'baseline' extra)"
135	        ) from exc
136	    if not rows:
137	        return np.empty((0, 0), dtype=float)
138	    return _cosine_ndarray(rows)
139	
140	
141	def cosine_similarity_matrix(rows: Sequence[Vector]) -> list[list[float]]:
142	    """Full symmetric cosine-similarity matrix (diagonal 1.0 for non-zero vectors, 0.0 for zero).
143	
144	    Pure-Python is O(n²·d) and does not scale (a corpus of thousands of embeddings would take
145	    minutes-to-hours); when numpy is available (the optional ``baseline`` extra) this dispatches
146	    to a vectorized path that is orders of magnitude faster. The two paths agree to within
147	    floating-point tolerance (both clamp to [-1, 1]); a study needing byte-identical
148	    reproducibility should pin its environment (the determinism harness records component
149	    versions for exactly this reason).
150	    """
151	    if not rows:
152	        return []
153	    try:
154	        import numpy  # noqa: F401
155	    except ImportError:
156	        return _cosine_matrix_pure(rows)
157	    return _cosine_matrix_numpy(rows)
158	
159	
160	def _quantile(sorted_values: list[float], q: float) -> float:
161	    """Linear-interpolation quantile (``q`` in [0, 1]) over already-sorted values."""
162	    if not 0.0 <= q <= 1.0:
163	        raise ValueError(f"quantile q must be in [0, 1]; got {q}")
164	    n = len(sorted_values)
165	    if n == 1:
166	        return sorted_values[0]
167	    pos = q * (n - 1)
168	    lo = math.floor(pos)
169	    hi = math.ceil(pos)

content.text: The freeze implementation confirms the docstring. Let me verify the exact details and check the git diff for this fix.

content.content:
82	def freeze(value: Any) -> Any:
83	    """Recursively convert ``value`` into an immutable, hashable form.
84	
85	    Mappings → :class:`FrozenDict`, lists/tuples → tuples, sets → frozensets. Known mutable
86	    buffers are converted to hashable equivalents (``bytearray`` → ``bytes``;
87	    ``array.array`` / numpy-like arrays exposing ``tobytes`` → a ``(kind, shape, bytes)``
88	    tuple). Any remaining leaf that is not hashable raises ``TypeError`` *here, at
89	    construction time* — rather than silently passing through and resurfacing the error the
90	    moment the record is hashed (audit pass 2, issue #19; the incomplete half of #7).
91	    """
92	    if isinstance(value, FrozenDict):
93	        return value
94	    if isinstance(value, Mapping):
95	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in value.items()})
96	    duck = duck_mapping_items(value)
97	    if duck is not None:  # lazy/proxy dict-like — freeze it like a mapping, not a leaf (#140)
98	        return FrozenDict({k: freeze(v) for k, v in duck})
99	    if isinstance(value, (list, tuple)):
100	        return tuple(freeze(v) for v in value)
101	    if isinstance(value, (set, frozenset)):
102	        return frozenset(freeze(v) for v in value)
103	    if isinstance(value, bytearray):
104	        return bytes(value)
105	    # Duck-typed buffer/array support (e.g. array.array, numpy.ndarray) without importing
106	    # numpy: an array-like exposing tobytes() is canonicalized to a hashable descriptor tuple.
107	    # Require an array-ish marker (shape/typecode/dtype) so an unrelated object that merely
108	    # happens to have a tobytes() is left to the hashability check below (fix-review nit, #19).
109	    tobytes = getattr(value, "tobytes", None)
110	    array_like = hasattr(value, "shape") or hasattr(value, "typecode") or hasattr(value, "dtype")
111	    if callable(tobytes) and array_like and not isinstance(value, (bytes, str)):
112	        shape = getattr(value, "shape", None)
113	        kind = getattr(getattr(value, "dtype", None), "str", None) or getattr(
114	            value, "typecode", type(value).__name__
115	        )
116	        # Route the descriptor through freeze() so a malformed tobytes() returning a
117	        # non-hashable value is still caught loud at construction (audit pass 3, issue #26).
118	        return freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape) if shape is not None else None, tobytes()))
119	    try:
120	        hash(value)
121	    except TypeError as exc:

commit 7dccfd8b5f8563c0da90d72ee92d954b99605422
Author: [REDACTED] <[REDACTED]>
Date:   Wed Jun 17 00:10:40 2026 -0400

    fix(hardening-p4): batch E — egress, error contracts, PII & test discrimination (8 findings)
    
    Pass-4 confirmed findings (2-agent verified, revert-discriminated):
    
    - G-007 (medium) the transcript scrubber redacts the bare operator username everywhere
      (git author strings, project-dir slugs, prose), not only inside /Users|/home paths.
    - G-018 (low) the high-stakes egress denylist now scrubs the ENTIRE ANTHROPIC_* namespace
      (any endpoint/auth/header/custom-header var) plus NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS /
      NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED (custom-CA / cert-bypass MITM enablers).
    - G-019 (low) Gate.evaluate type-guards the precondition's return (non-GateDecision ->
      typed GateError), the gate analogue of the #F-021 Verdict guards.
    - G-020 (low) make_gate_node records to the trail before returning the state update and, on
      a recorder failure, raises a clear GateError instead of returning the update — so graph
      state can't diverge from the tamper-evident trail.
    - G-021 (low) build_baseline_artifact names the offending extra_input key when it isn't
      canonicalizable (was a raw, context-free ValueError/TypeError from hash_value).
    - G-024 (low) corrected the cosine_similarity_matrix_array docstring: the determinism freeze
      copies the array bytes once (O(size)), it is NOT zero-copy/O(1).
    - G-013 (medium) test-discrimination: the cosine fast-path test now asserts dispatch to the
      numpy path AND numpy==pure agreement (a revert to always-pure now fails).
    - G-014 (medium) test-discrimination: the SIGTERM flush test asserts the handler is installed
      unconditionally on the main thread (was gated on `if "h" in captured`, silently skippable).
    
    Gates: ruff + ruff format --check + mypy --strict clean; 520 passed, 5 skipped.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.8 (1M context) <[REDACTED]>

content.text:
This is a doc-accuracy fix. The fix is verified against the actual implementation:

**Old (incorrect) docstring:** claimed "the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1)".

**New (corrected) docstring:** "The determinism freeze still copies the array's bytes once (`freeze` -> `tobytes()` into a `(kind, shape, bytes)` descriptor, O(size)) ... but NOT zero-copy/O(1)".

Checking against the actual `freeze` implementation in `/Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/_frozen.py` lines 109-118:
- Line 109-111: detects array-like via `tobytes` + shape/typecode/dtype marker — matches docstring.
- Line 113-115: `kind` from `dtype.str` or `typecode` — matches `(kind, ...)`.
- Line 118: `freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape)..., tobytes()))` — produces exactly the `(kind, shape, bytes)` descriptor, and `tobytes()` materializes a full byte copy → O(size), not zero-copy/O(1). 

The corrected docstring is precisely accurate. The relative claim ("far cheaper than freezing a deep nested list element-by-element") is also correct: the nested-list path recurses element-by-element (line 99-100) which is O(n²) Python-object work, versus a single C-level `tobytes()`.

For a doc-accuracy item there is no code-behavior test to discriminate; the "(doc-accuracy)" test designation correctly reflects that. Judging on correctness/coverage: the docstring now matches the implementation exactly. No regression — only a comment changed.

input.reasoning:
Doc-accuracy fix in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/baseline/primitives.py (cosine_similarity_matrix_array docstring, lines 125-127). The old docstring falsely claimed "the determinism freeze keeps an array opaque/O(1)". The corrected text states the freeze "still copies the array's bytes once (freeze -> tobytes() into a (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor, O(size)) ... but NOT zero-copy/O(1)".

I verified this against the actual implementation in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/src/assay_engine/_frozen.py (freeze, lines 109-118): an array-like object (detected via tobytes + shape/typecode/dtype marker) is canonicalized via freeze((str(kind), tuple(shape), tobytes())). tobytes() materializes a full byte copy of the array — exactly O(size), not zero-copy/O(1) — and the result is precisely the (kind, shape, bytes) descriptor the docstring names. The comparative claim (far cheaper than freezing a nested list element-by-element) is also correct: the list path recurses per element (lines 99-100) whereas the array path is one C-level tobytes() call.

Appropriate: the new docstring matches the code exactly; the change is comment-only with zero behavioral/regression risk. Complete: this is a pure doc-accuracy item with no runtime behavior to assert, so revert-discrimination via a test is not applicable (correctly labeled "(doc-accuracy)"); the docstring fully and accurately closes the inaccurate complexity claim. No remaining stale O(1)/zero-copy assertions elsewhere in the file.

content.text: Verified G-024 as CONFIRMED — the corrected docstring accurately matches the actual `freeze` implementation (O(size) byte copy via `tobytes()` into a `(kind, shape, bytes)` descriptor, not zero-copy/O(1)), it is a comment-only change with no regression risk, and as a doc-accuracy item revert-discrimination via test is not applicable.
